content.text: When an AI agent executes code on behalf of a user or another system, that code can do almost anything the host operating system allows — read files, make network requests, fork processes, or exfiltrate data. Execution sandboxing is the practice of running that code inside a constrained environment where every dangerous capability is explicitly restricted or denied. It is the primary technical control that separates a safely deployable code-execution agent from one that is a serious liability.

Execution sandboxing places untrusted or semi-trusted code inside a hardened container where it runs with a radically reduced set of privileges. The sandbox intercepts or outright blocks system calls, filesystem operations, network connections, and inter-process communication that fall outside a pre-approved policy. From the sandbox's perspective the code appears to run normally; from the host's perspective only a tightly bounded set of observable effects can escape the boundary.

In agentic AI systems, sandboxing is applied whenever an agent generates and runs code — for data analysis, tool use, autonomous script execution, code-interpreter features, or multi-agent workflows where one agent produces code another agent evaluates. The untrusted code is the agent-generated snippet; the trusted context is the orchestrator, host filesystem, secrets store, and any downstream services. Sandboxing keeps the blast radius of a malicious, buggy, or prompt-injected code generation strictly contained.

A sandbox is defined by its policy — the enumeration of what is allowed rather than what is forbidden. Common policy dimensions include: which filesystem paths are readable or writable, which system calls may be invoked, how much CPU and memory the process may consume, whether outbound network connections are permitted (and to which hosts), and how long the process may run before being terminated. The policy is enforced by a kernel-level or hypervisor-level mechanism so that user-space code inside the sandbox cannot bypass it, regardless of what tricks the code attempts.

Code-execution capability is one of the highest-value and highest-risk features an AI agent can have. Without sandboxing, every invocation of agent-generated code is a potential supply-chain attack, a credential theft, a ransomware vector, or an exfiltration channel. The risks are not theoretical: researchers have demonstrated that large language models can be prompted — or prompt-injected through external content — to emit code that deletes files, calls home to attacker-controlled servers, or pivots laterally through internal networks.
— an agent reading a webpage or document can encounter embedded instructions telling it to exfiltrate data; sandboxing blocks the exfiltration path even if the injection succeeds.
— agent-generated code may pip-install or npm-install malicious packages; network isolation and filesystem restrictions limit what those packages can do.
— an infinite loop or a memory-hungry algorithm in agent output can crash the host without CPU and memory cgroups.
— once code runs on a host that has access to internal services, network isolation inside the sandbox prevents horizontal pivoting.
— without sandboxing, agent code can read ~/.ssh, environment variables containing API keys, or mounted secrets volumes and send them externally.
Defense in depth against model failures
— even when the model produces malicious or buggy code, the sandbox limits the damage to a narrow, recoverable scope.
— each invocation starts from a known image, eliminating state contamination between runs and making debugging deterministic.
— a declared seccomp profile, cgroup configuration, or OCI runtime spec is a machine-readable, version-controllable security contract that can be reviewed and tested independently of the agent.
— cgroup limits prevent one user's code execution from starving other users of CPU and memory.
— sandboxed execution is a prerequisite for processing regulated data under frameworks like SOC 2, ISO 27001, and HIPAA, where untrusted code must not be able to access PII or PHI at rest.

— spinning up a container or microVM for each code execution adds 100–2000 ms of overhead, which can be prohibitive for interactive use cases. Warm pooling mitigates this but adds operational complexity.
— an overly permissive seccomp profile or a missing network rule gives a false sense of security. The policy must be audited and tested as rigorously as the code itself.
Side-channel vulnerabilities at the kernel level
— container-based sandboxes share the host kernel; kernel exploits (e.g., Dirty Pipe) can escape the namespace boundary. Stronger isolation (microVMs, gVisor) reduces this risk but increases latency.
Restricted capability can confuse the model
— if the sandbox blocks a system call the model's code legitimately needs, the agent receives a cryptic error and may loop, retrying or generating workaround code that escalates privilege.
— sandboxing controls what code can do while running; it does not sanitize the text the code writes to stdout, which could contain injected instructions for the next LLM call.
— ephemeral sandboxes cannot easily share state between agent turns without an explicit external store, which adds architecture complexity.

Execution sandboxing combines multiple independent kernel and hypervisor mechanisms, layered so that breaking one does not expose the host. The following subsections describe the four primary enforcement planes.

Linux namespaces partition kernel resources so that processes inside a namespace see only their own slice. The PID namespace gives the sandboxed process a private process tree (PID 1 is its own init, not the host init). The network namespace provides an isolated network stack with no routes to the host by default. Mount namespaces control which filesystem paths are visible; a read-only bind mount of the host root combined with a writable

means code can write temporary files but cannot persist them or access host paths. User namespaces allow the process to appear to run as root inside the sandbox while mapping to an unprivileged UID on the host.
cgroups v2 impose hard resource limits on the process tree inside the sandbox.
triggers an OOM kill if the process exceeds the memory ceiling;
prevents fork bombs. These limits are enforced by the kernel scheduler independently of what the process attempts, making them impossible to bypass from user space.

The secure computing mode (seccomp) attaches a BPF filter to the process that is evaluated on every system call. If the call number or its arguments do not match the allowlist, the kernel either kills the process (SECCOMP_RET_KILL) or returns an error (SECCOMP_RET_ERRNO). A typical code-execution sandbox allows read/write on pre-opened file descriptors, basic memory allocation, process exit, and clock access, while denying
. Seccomp operates at the syscall boundary so it cannot be bypassed by any user-space technique.

For stronger isolation, the sandboxed process can run inside a lightweight virtual machine rather than sharing the host kernel. Google's gVisor intercepts system calls in a user-space kernel (the Sentry) written in Go; the guest process never makes direct Linux kernel calls. Firecracker (AWS) runs each sandbox inside a KVM microVM with a separate kernel and less than 5 MB of memory overhead per VM. MicroVM isolation eliminates kernel-sharing side channels at the cost of higher startup latency (50–300 ms vs. <10 ms for a namespace-only sandbox).

A wall-clock timeout is enforced outside the sandbox by the orchestrator. When the deadline expires, the orchestrator sends SIGKILL to the sandbox's PID (or tears down the container/VM). This prevents an infinite loop or a long-running computation from blocking the agent indefinitely. Typical timeouts for interactive code-interpreter use cases are 10–30 seconds; batch data analysis might allow 5–10 minutes.

attrs.alt: Execution Sandboxing Architecture — showing the AI agent submitting code to the policy enforcement layer, which passes it to the isolated execution environment (filesystem, process space, network, runtime), with escaped output blocked and sanitized stdout returned to the agent.

content.text: The diagram shows the one-way data path: the orchestrator submits code inward through the policy enforcement layer, the runtime executes it inside the isolated environment, and only captured stdout flows back out. Attempted escapes — accessing host filesystems, making unapproved network connections, or calling blocked syscalls — are intercepted at the policy layer and never reach the host kernel or network.

Think of the sandbox as a bank's pneumatic tube system. The teller (orchestrator) sends a canister (code) down the tube to the vault area (sandbox). Inside the vault, clerks (runtime processes) open the canister and do the requested work. The clerks have access only to what is inside the vault — they cannot open fire exits, use telephones to call outside, or take anything home. The only thing that travels back up the tube is the receipt (stdout). The tube itself (policy enforcement layer) ensures nothing unauthorized can ride along.

This model makes the key insight clear: the attack surface is the output tube, not the vault itself. Code inside the sandbox can compute anything, but the only influence it can have on the outside world is through the text it writes to stdout. That output is treated as untrusted data by the orchestrator — it is not executed directly but passed through an output sanitizer before being fed to the next LLM call or stored.
— any agent feature that accepts natural-language requests and executes generated Python, JavaScript, or shell must sandbox every execution.
— agents that have a bash or PowerShell tool need sandboxed execution even for seemingly benign commands.
Multi-agent pipelines where code is passed between agents
— when one agent produces code that another agent evaluates, that evaluation step must sandbox the run.
— agent-generated pandas or SQL code running over PII data must be sandboxed and network-isolated to prevent exfiltration.
— AI-generated test code or build scripts must not be able to access deployment credentials or modify source control.

— if the agent operates on non-code artifacts only (text, structured data, images) with no code-execution step, sandboxing adds overhead with no security benefit. Do not apply it at the model-inference layer; apply it specifically at the code-execution layer.
The following steps describe building a container-based execution sandbox using Docker and the Python
SDK. The same pattern applies to Podman, containerd, or any OCI-compatible runtime.
Use a distroless or Alpine-based image with only the required runtime (Python, Node) and no shell or package manager. Fewer installed tools means fewer attack surfaces.
Start from Docker's default seccomp profile and remove any syscalls not needed by the runtime. At minimum, block
on the container. Memory and PID limits prevent denial-of-service from runaway or fork-bombing code.
and re-add only the capabilities required (usually none for pure computation). This eliminates privilege escalation vectors.
unless the code legitimately needs network access, in which case use a restricted bridge with egress firewall rules.
with a size limit. Make the root filesystem read-only with
directive set to UID 1000 or higher. Pass
at runtime for an extra enforcement layer.
Wrap the container run in a timeout in the orchestrator. Kill the container with

import docker
import time

client = docker.from_env()

def run_sandboxed(code: str, timeout_seconds: int = 15) -> dict:
    """
    Execute agent-generated Python code in a Docker sandbox.
    Returns {"stdout": str, "exit_code": int, "timed_out": bool}.
    """
    # Wrap user code in a minimal runner
    script = f"""import sys, traceback
try:
    exec(compile('''{code}''', '<agent>', 'exec'))
except Exception as e:
    print(f'ERROR: {{traceback.format_exc()}}', file=sys.stderr)
"""
    container = client.containers.run(
        image="python:3.12-alpine",
        command=["python", "-c", script],
        detach=True,
        # Resource limits
        mem_limit="128m",
        nano_cpus=500_000_000,   # 0.5 CPU
        pids_limit=32,
        # Isolation
        network_mode="none",
        read_only=True,
        tmpfs={"/tmp": "size=32m"},
        cap_drop=["ALL"],
        security_opt=["no-new-privileges:true"],
        user="1000:1000",
    )

    timed_out = False
    try:
        container.wait(timeout=timeout_seconds)
    except Exception:
        timed_out = True
        container.kill()

    logs = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False).decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
    exit_code = container.attrs.get("State", {}).get("ExitCode", -1) if not timed_out else -1
    container.remove(force=True)

    return {"stdout": logs[:8192], "exit_code": exit_code, "timed_out": timed_out}
Example 1 — Minimal seccomp profile for a Python sandbox
A seccomp profile is a JSON document listing allowed syscalls. The following is an abbreviated allowlist for a pure-computation Python process with no networking, no file writes outside
are absent from the allowlist — any attempt to call them returns EPERM rather than killing the process, so the agent receives an error it can retry gracefully.
Example 2 — Sandbox runner with cgroup v2 via systemd-run
On systems with a cgroup v2-enabled kernel,
can create a transient scope with tight resource limits without requiring a container runtime.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Run agent-generated Python code in a systemd transient scope with cgroup limits.
# Usage: run_sandboxed.sh <script_file>

SCRIPT="$1"
TIMEOUT=15

systemd-run \
  --scope \
  --user \
  --property=MemoryMax=128M \
  --property=CPUQuota=50% \
  --property=TasksMax=16 \
  --property=RuntimeMaxSec=$TIMEOUT \
  -- python3 "$SCRIPT" 2>/dev/null

# Exit code propagates from the python process.
# stdout is the only permitted output channel.
Example 3 — gVisor (runsc) container for stronger kernel isolation

# Install gVisor runtime (runsc) and register it with Docker:
# https://gvisor.dev/docs/user_guide/install/

# Run a sandboxed container using the gVisor runtime.
# The process's syscalls go to gVisor's Sentry (user-space kernel),
# not directly to the Linux kernel.
docker run \
  --runtime=runsc \
  --memory=128m \
  --cpus=0.5 \
  --pids-limit=32 \
  --network=none \
  --read-only \
  --tmpfs /tmp:size=32m \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --security-opt no-new-privileges \
  --user 1000:1000 \
  python:3.12-alpine \
  python -c "print(sum(range(1_000_000)))"

A fintech platform uses an AI assistant that lets analysts request custom portfolio analyses in natural language. The assistant generates pandas + NumPy code to compute metrics over the user's holdings data. The holdings data contains PII and is loaded from a read-only bind mount inside the sandbox. The sandbox network is set to

so the generated code cannot POST the CSV to an external endpoint. Each analysis runs in an ephemeral container that is torn down immediately after; no state persists between analyst sessions. cgroup memory is capped at 512 MB per container; the entire fleet is auto-scaled on ECS Fargate so container density does not affect the host's operational stability.

import docker
import json
import tempfile
import os

def analyze_portfolio(agent_code: str, holdings_csv_path: str) -> str:
    """
    Run agent-generated analysis code against the user's holdings CSV.
    The CSV is bind-mounted read-only; no network access; output capped at 16 KB.
    """
    client = docker.from_env()

    # Write the agent code to a temp file (never shell-escape it; pass via mount)
    with tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile(mode='w', suffix='.py', delete=False) as f:
        f.write(agent_code)
        script_path = f.name

    try:
        container = client.containers.run(
            image="fintech-analysis-sandbox:latest",   # pre-built with pandas, numpy
            command=["python", "/sandbox/script.py"],
            volumes={
                script_path: {"bind": "/sandbox/script.py", "mode": "ro"},
                holdings_csv_path: {"bind": "/data/holdings.csv", "mode": "ro"},
            },
            environment={"HOLDINGS_PATH": "/data/holdings.csv"},
            detach=True,
            mem_limit="512m",
            nano_cpus=1_000_000_000,   # 1 CPU
            pids_limit=64,
            network_mode="none",
            read_only=True,
            tmpfs={"/tmp": "size=64m"},
            cap_drop=["ALL"],
            security_opt=["no-new-privileges:true"],
            user="1000:1000",
        )

        result = container.wait(timeout=30)
        stdout = container.logs(stdout=True, stderr=False).decode("utf-8", errors="replace")
        container.remove(force=True)

        # Truncate output to prevent prompt-injection through stdout
        return stdout[:16384]

    finally:
        os.unlink(script_path)
— if the code escapes the namespace (e.g., via a kernel exploit), it runs as root on the host. Always set
Passing user code via environment variables or command arguments
— environment variables are visible to all processes and can be read via
. Always write the code to a file and mount it read-only, or pipe it via stdin.
— a sandbox that returns unlimited output allows code to write arbitrarily large payloads back to the orchestrator, wasting tokens or injecting prompt-manipulation content.
— if a container is reused, files written to
by one execution are visible to the next. Each execution must use a fresh container.
Using privileged mode or mounting the Docker socket
gives the container the same capabilities as the host. Mounting
allows escape via the Docker API. Neither should ever appear in a sandbox specification.
Relying on timeout alone for resource safety
— a timeout alone does not prevent memory exhaustion before the timeout fires. Combine the timeout with a
Treating container isolation as equivalent to microVM isolation
— OCI containers share the host kernel. For high-security or multi-tenant production deployments, use gVisor or Firecracker for kernel isolation.
Use allowlist-based seccomp profiles, not denylist.
A denylist must enumerate every dangerous syscall; an attacker only needs to find one that was missed. An allowlist starts empty and adds only what is proven necessary.
Pre-warm a pool of ready containers.
Cold-starting a container for every agent turn adds 300–800 ms latency. Keep a pool of idle, clean containers and swap them in for execution, then teardown and replenish after each use.
Immutable base images with pinned digests.
in the Dockerfile to guarantee the execution environment never silently changes due to a tag update.
Sanitize stdout before passing to the LLM.
Truncate length, strip ANSI escape codes, and consider running stdout through the same input validation as any other external content before feeding it to the next model call.
Log all executions with the original code.
Store the agent-generated code, the sandbox policy applied, resource usage, stdout, and exit code in an append-only audit log. This is essential for debugging and for post-incident forensics.
Test the seccomp profile with strace before production.
in a dev environment to enumerate the syscalls the runtime actually needs, then build the profile from that list.
If the container runtime fails to start, the policy fails to apply, or any security option is rejected, the execution must be aborted entirely — not fallen back to an unsandboxed mode.

The dominant latency cost in execution sandboxing is container startup time. On a warm Docker daemon with the image already pulled, a new container can be ready in 50–200 ms for namespace-only isolation, or 300–1000 ms for a Firecracker microVM. gVisor's Sentry initialization adds another 50–200 ms. For synchronous, interactive agent use cases (target P99 response time under 2 s), pre-warmed container pools are not optional — they are a design requirement.
Memory overhead per sandbox slot depends on the runtime image. A

container occupies roughly 50 MB RSS before any user code runs. With a 512 MB limit per container, a machine with 32 GB RAM can safely host about 60 concurrent slots with headroom for the orchestrator and OS. Firecracker microVMs have a minimum overhead of about 5 MB for the VMM itself plus the guest kernel footprint (~20 MB), making them memory-efficient relative to full-weight VMs.

Horizontal scaling for sandboxed code execution is straightforward: each execution is stateless and ephemeral, so the fleet scales as a pool of identical workers behind a queue. ECS Fargate, Kubernetes Jobs, or AWS Lambda (with 15-minute timeout removed) all handle sandbox worker scaling well. The primary tuning knobs are container pool size per node, maximum execution memory, and the number of concurrent active containers per CPU core. On a 4-core node, running more than 8–12 concurrent CPU-bound containers causes scheduler contention and inflates P99 latency.
Used in AWS Lambda; strong isolation
Designed for LLM code execution; SDK available
Serverless compute; good Python ecosystem support
Extremely fast; language-limited; no native code
Python in browser or Node; no subprocess; limited stdlib
Plugins/extensions; not yet suitable for all runtimes
Language-level approaches like RestrictedPython or static AST analysis are not reliable sandboxes for production agentic systems — they can be bypassed through import tricks, C extensions, or
. They may have a place in educational or low-risk settings but should never be the sole security control for agent-generated code.

Execution sandboxing is itself a security control, so its failure modes must be considered as part of the threat model. The most significant risk is a container escape through a kernel vulnerability. The Linux kernel CVE history shows that namespace and seccomp bypasses do occur (roughly 2–4 CVEs per year that could theoretically enable escape from a well-configured container). The mitigation is layered defense: run on a recent kernel with all security patches applied, use gVisor or Firecracker for sensitive workloads to eliminate kernel sharing, keep the base image minimal, and monitor for anomalous syscall patterns in production using tools like Falco.
— treat the host kernel as a critical security surface. Automate OS patching and reboot the sandbox fleet within 24–48 hours of a kernel security release.
— tools like Falco monitor kernel events and alert on suspicious patterns inside containers: unexpected syscall sequences,
reads, or network socket creation in a no-network container (indicating seccomp misconfiguration).
— use Docker Content Trust or Cosign to verify that the container image pulled at runtime is the one built and signed by the CI pipeline, not a tampered version from a registry attack.

— the orchestrator must handle OOM kills and timeout exits gracefully, returning a structured error to the agent rather than crashing. The agent should retry with simplified code or request clarification rather than silently dropping the result.
— validate that stdout is valid UTF-8 (or the expected encoding), within the size limit, and free of known injection patterns before passing it back to the LLM context window.

— the sandbox is one layer in a broader security architecture. It complements — and does not replace — prompt-level safeguards, output classifiers, role-based access control on data, and audit logging.

Execution sandboxing restricts agent-generated code to a controlled environment using Linux namespaces, cgroups, seccomp syscall filtering, and capability restrictions; breaking any one layer still leaves the others intact.
The policy must be allowlist-based — enumerate what the runtime legitimately needs and deny everything else — because denylist policies always miss edge cases.
Container sandboxes share the host Linux kernel; for high-security or adversarial workloads, microVM isolation (Firecracker) or a user-space kernel (gVisor) eliminates this shared surface.
Pre-warmed container pools are necessary for interactive agent use cases; cold-start latency of 100–1000 ms per execution is unacceptable at conversational response times.
The output path (stdout returned to the orchestrator) is the primary remaining attack surface; it must be length-capped and treated as untrusted content before being fed into the next LLM call.

Never run the sandbox process as root, never use privileged mode, and never mount the Docker socket inside the sandbox — these three mistakes alone account for the majority of container escape incidents.

Execution sandboxing is a necessary but not sufficient control: it must be combined with prompt-level safeguards, output validation, data access controls, and runtime anomaly detection to form a complete agentic security posture.
